figures.caption: Figure 2: Overview of the SynFlash generation pipeline. ImageNet-1k [48] images are injected at random times into videos formed by concatenating three Panda-70M [49] clips, creating four transient anomaly types with frame-level ground truth.
Figure 10: Case study of PiP injection pattern.
Figure 3: Case study from SynFlash (PiP). Left: sampled frames from different methods. Right: example questions.

Figure 5: Similarity matrices on SynFlash under different feature extractors. The first row uses HSV colorhistogram features and the second row uses semantic features. Each column shows one example video from a SynFlash subset (Camouflage, High-Sat, Curtain, and PiP). Red arrows indicate the temporal injected flash event.

Table 4: Ablation on the feature extractor used by InfoShot on SynFlash under a fixed budget (0.5 fps). Metrics include frame-level Recall (R, %), Anomaly Retrieval (AR, %), and Temporal Ordering (TO, %). Best results are highlighted in bold, and second-best are underlined.

Figure 1: The overall framework of our approach. We propose a task-agnostic and plug-and-play sampler, InfoShot, before the VLM to select keyframes under a fixed budget. InfoShot performs greedy shot segmentation in deep feature space (Sec. 3.4) and then selects two frames per shot: a common and a unique high-deviation frames (Sec. 3.5). The downstream VLM uses the same prompt; only the sampled frame set differs.
Figure 7: Case study of camouflage injection pattern.

Table 1: Performance comparison on SynFlash. Metrics include frame-level Recall (R, %), Anomaly Retrieval Accuracy (AR, %), and Temporal Ordering Accuracy (TO, %). Best results are highlighted in bold, and second-best are underlined.
Figure 8: Case study of high-saturation injection pattern.
Figure 6: SynFlash evaluation results with different methods (same metrics as above) as function of fps, x-axis.
Figure 9: Case study of curtain injection pattern.
